hook: Mountain temple of winning luck, carpeted in thousands of stubby daruma dolls
facts: Founded as a hermitage in the 8th century; renamed after Emperor Seiwa's recovery was credited to its monks
Worshipped for over 1,300 years as a temple of katsu, victory in exams, business, sport and elections
Visitors leave numberless small daruma dolls wedged into every ledge, lantern and tree root
Sits in the Minoo hills north of the city, far above the suburbs
legend: Devotees paint in a daruma's second eye only when their wish has come true, then return the doll to the temple
w: A mountain temple north of the city where katsu means victory, so the faithful come for luck in exams, lawsuits, even elections. Win, and you paint in your daruma's second eye and bring it back. The grounds drown in thousands of the stubby red dolls, jammed into every ledge, root and gutter. An army of squat optimists.

hook: Wooden tenement quarter that dodged the firebombs, now full of folk-house cafes
facts: Dense wooden nagaya rowhouses from the Taisho and early Showa eras still stand
Largely spared the 1945 air raids that flattened most of surrounding Osaka
Revival began around 2001 when artists took the cheap, atmospheric old houses
Sits a short walk from the Umeda skyscrapers, a different century entirely
w: A warren of pre-war wooden tenements that somehow slipped through the 1945 firebombing while the rest of Osaka burned. For decades it just aged, then around 2001 artists colonised the cheap nagaya rowhouses and made them cafes and galleries. Lanes too narrow for cars, minutes from the Umeda towers. Another century, left running.

hook: Old furniture-makers' street reborn as a low-slung boutique strip
facts: Horie grew around a timber canal dug in 1688 for Osaka's lumber trade
Tachibana-dori became the postwar furniture town, rebuilt after the 1945 bombing
Nicknamed Orange Street after tachibana, a mandarin variety
Old furniture workshops now sit beside fashion ateliers and cafes
w: For generations this street smelled of cedar sawdust, Osaka's furniture town, rebuilt by returning families after the 1945 bombs. The cabinet-makers thinned out, but a few prewar timber shops still trade among the boutiques that arrived in the 1990s. They call it Orange Street, after a mandarin. Sawhorses and selvedge denim, side by side.

hook: Vast art warehouse and mural district on a dead shipyard
facts: Kitakagaya bustled with shipbuilding until the 1970s, then emptied out
Former factories and warehouses now hold artist studios, galleries and murals
MASK fills a former steel plant with oversized works too big for normal galleries
Around 130 artists work across some 40 sites in the neighbourhood
w: When the shipyards died in the 1970s, this bayside grid went silent, all empty sheds and rusting cranes. Artists drifted in for the space and the rent. Now murals climb the warehouse walls and a former steel plant, christened MASK, swallows artworks too vast for any normal gallery. A whole dead industry handed over to the painters.

hook: Faded Showa drinking alley of kushikatsu, shogi parlours and shamisen ghosts
facts: Opened in 1921 in the boom days of Shinsekai's amusement quarter
Name mimics janjan, the twang of shamisen once played to lure customers
Lined with kushikatsu counters, oden bars and old go and shogi game parlours
Bombed flat in the war, revived feeding Expo 70 labourers, restored in 1997
w: The name copies janjan, the twang of the shamisen once strummed to reel drinkers off the street back in 1921. Bombed flat, rebuilt to feed Expo 70's labourers, it still runs on kushikatsu, oden and old men hunched over go and shogi boards. Paint faded, lanterns smoke-stained, the swagger intact. Showa Osaka, still serving.

hook: Ancient sea-god shrine with a near-vertical drum bridge and a fortune-telling stone
facts: Head of roughly 2,300 Sumiyoshi shrines across Japan, dedicated to sea deities
The Sorihashi arched bridge climbs at a punishing 48 degrees, rebuilt around 1600
Crossing the bridge counts as an act of ritual purification
An omokaru-ishi stone tells fortunes by whether it feels lighter on the second lift
legend: Lift the omokaru stone, make a wish, lift again; lighter means the wish will come true, heavier means keep trying
w: Mother shrine to some 2,300 Sumiyoshi shrines, guarding sailors since the city was islands in a bay. Its Sorihashi bridge rears up at a vicious 48 degrees, crossing it counts as purification, and the wood turns lethal in rain. Lift the omokaru stone, wish, lift again: lighter means yes. Worship as a small test of nerve.

n: Osaka Museum of Housing and Living
hook: A full Edo-period Osaka street rebuilt indoors on the ninth floor
facts: Recreates a life-size 1830s Osaka townscape on the museum's ninth floor
Lighting and sound cycle through a full day, dusk, rain, even cats on the roof
Visitors stroll the townhouses, alleys and shops and can rent yukata
Faces the Tenjinbashisuji arcade in Kita-ku
w: Step out of a lift onto a whole 1830s Osaka street, full-size, rebuilt indoors. The ceiling fakes a sky that drifts from dawn to dusk to a sudden rainstorm, while cats sit on the roofs and lanterns glow over the toy shop. You can wander the alleys in a rented yukata. A city two centuries gone, switched on by the hour.

hook: Moss-furred deity drenched daily with water by hopeful hands
facts: Hozenji dates to 1637, once hosting rakugo and stage plays for the theatre district
Its Fudo Myoo statue stays soaked, so moss covers it head to toe
Visitors splash water over the statue after praying, for luck
Sits at the end of stone-paved Hozenji Yokocho, rebuilt after 1945 bombing
legend: Said a desperate woman once poured water on the statue's feet in her prayers and her wish came true, so the practice spread
w: A deity drowned in moss because everyone keeps throwing water at it. The Fudo Myoo of Hozenji, from 1637, takes a ladleful over the head from each supplicant, until the stone vanished under green felt. Legend pins the habit on one desperate woman whose soaked prayer was answered. Down a stone lane off neon Namba, the kami stays damp.

hook: Shrine with a 12-metre lion's head that swallows bad luck whole
facts: The giant lion-head stage was built in 1975, 12 metres tall
Its eyes work as lights and its nose as a loudspeaker
Believed to gulp down evil spirits and grant luck in study and business
Enshrines Susanoo, part of a temple complex burned in wartime air raids
legend: The open jaws are said to swallow misfortune and bring victory to those who stand before them
w: A shrine where a 12-metre lion head glowers over the courtyard, jaws agape to gulp down bad luck. Built in 1975, eyes wired as floodlights, nose doubling as a speaker, it looks beamed in from a theme park yet stands dead serious about swallowing your misfortune. Stand in the maw and ask for luck. Faith, gone gloriously large.

hook: Retro tower guarded by a grinning American luck-god gone native
facts: Billiken, the God of Things As They Ought to Be, was dreamed up by an American illustrator in 1908
A statue arrived in Shinsekai's Luna Park in 1912, then vanished, then returned
The current Billiken sits in the rebuilt 1956 tower's observatory
Visitors rub his feet for luck so often the soles get replaced
legend: Rubbing the soles of Billiken's feet is said to bring good fortune
w: Osaka's mascot god of luck, the grinning pointy-headed Billiken, came not from a temple but from an American illustrator's 1908 dream. He landed in Shinsekai's Luna Park in 1912, vanished, then resurfaced in the rebuilt tower's observatory. Pilgrims rub his feet so hard the soles wear through. The God of Things As They Ought to Be.

hook: Temple whose Buddhas are moulded from the ashes of the dead
facts: Founded by Honen as a hermitage in 1185, near Shitennoji
Makes okotsubutsu, Buddha statues built from cremated human remains
The first, in 1887, used the bones of some 50,000 people; a new one every decade since
Six wartime statues were lost when bombing destroyed the temple
w: Here the Buddhas are made of people. Since 1887 the monks have worked the donated ashes of the dead into okotsubutsu, statues holding the remains of tens of thousands at a time, a fresh one cast every decade. Six were lost to wartime bombs; the count goes on. Worship and the worshipped, the same substance at last. Devotion, made literal.

hook: Forest waterfall where vendors deep-fry maple leaves for the autumn crowds
facts: A 33-metre fall at the head of a wooded valley north of the city
Reached by a 2.7-km riverside trail through one of Japan's older parks
Momiji tempura, battered and fried maple leaves, has been sold here for over a century
Momotaro souvenir shop began selling the fried leaves around 1901
legend: The maple-frying custom links back to the mountain ascetic En no Gyoja, who supposedly trained in these hills
w: Walk a wooded river gorge north of the city and you reach a 33-metre fall, and a snack found almost nowhere else: maple leaves, salted for a year, battered and deep-fried into sweet crisps. Shops here have sold momiji tempura since around 1901, the habit traced to mountain ascetics. A waterfall, and a forest you can eat.

hook: Japan's oldest state temple, ringed by a pond of abandoned pet turtles
facts: Founded in 593 by Prince Shotoku, among the oldest temples in Japan
Repeatedly burned and rebuilt over fourteen centuries
Kame-no-ike pond fills with turtles, many once dumped pets the monks took in
A huge flea market fills the grounds on the 21st and 22nd of each month
w: Prince Shotoku founded this temple in 593, about the oldest in Japan, burned and rebuilt more times than anyone counts. The quiet surprise sits in a side pond: scores of turtles, most of them unwanted pets dumped and then adopted by the monks. Twice a month a flea market swamps the grounds. Ancient, and overrun with reptiles.

hook: Century-old city zoo, the only place in Japan to see a drill
facts: Opened on New Year's Day 1915, the third zoo built in Japan
Home to roughly 1,000 animals across 170 species on 11 hectares
Keeps drills, jet-black-faced monkeys found in no other Japanese zoo
Tucked into Tennoji Park between the skyscrapers and Shinsekai
w: Open since New Year's Day 1915, the country's third zoo, wedged between the Tennoji towers and the smoke of Shinsekai. Among the lions and bears it keeps a creature no other Japanese zoo has: the drill, a glowering, jet-black-faced monkey rarely held anywhere. A hundred years old, and quietly hoarding the rarest face in the country.

hook: War-spared shopping lane of machiya rowhouses turned cafes
facts: Bustled from the Meiji era and largely escaped wartime air raids
Keeps prewar machiya townhouses and narrow alleys on gentle slopes
Architects have renovated old rowhouses into shops and cafes since the 1990s
Bookcafe Mori occupies a building from 1907, among Karahori's oldest
w: One of the few Osaka quarters the bombers missed, so the wooden machiya and crooked sloping lanes survived into a city of concrete. From the 1990s local architects coaxed cafes and craft shops from the old rowhouses, one a bookcafe in a frame standing since 1907. Walk the slopes and the prewar town reassembles around you. Quietly intact.

hook: Lovers' shrine named for a courtesan who died here in a famous double suicide
facts: Founded around 1,300 years ago, when Sonezaki was a small bay island
In 1703 a courtesan, Ohatsu, and her lover Tokubei killed themselves in its grove
Chikamatsu turned the tragedy into the bunraku play The Love Suicides at Sonezaki
Now a matchmaking shrine wedged among Umeda's bars and offices
legend: So many couples copied the play's double suicide that the shogunate banned such plays in 1722
w: In 1703 a courtesan named Ohatsu and her doomed clerk Tokubei died together in this shrine's grove. Within weeks Chikamatsu staged it as a puppet play, and copycat lovers followed in such numbers the shogun banned the genre. The shrine took the dead woman's name and now draws couples praying for love. Romance and ruin, one ground.

hook: Quiet park hillock that was the bloody pivot of the siege of Osaka
facts: Low mound in Tennoji Park, possibly an ancient burial tumulus
Served as a command post in the 1614 to 1615 Battle of Osaka
Samurai hero Sanada Yukimura made his last stand near here in 1615
Stone monuments at the summit mark the campaign
w: A grassy bump in a city park, easy to walk past, yet warlords once fought over it. Possibly an old burial mound, Chausuyama became a headquarters in the 1615 siege of Osaka, where doomed Sanada Yukimura made his last charge before falling nearby. Now pensioners feed pigeons on the slope. The hinge of a dynasty, gone to lawn.

hook: Red-brick Taisho palace paid for by a broker who later went bust
facts: Opened in 1918 on Nakanoshima island, Neo-Renaissance in red brick and stone
Funded by stockbroker Einosuke Iwamoto, inspired by American philanthropy
Designed via competition by the young Shinichiro Okada, then aged 29
Restored 1999 to 2002 and named an Important Cultural Property
w: A broker named Iwamoto, moved by American philanthropy, handed over a million yen to build the city a grand hall, then lost his fortune speculating and reportedly took his own life before it opened in 1918. The red-brick Taisho pile still presides over Nakanoshima, all domes and stained glass. Generosity in brick, with a tragic footnote.

hook: Last smart-ball parlour in Osaka, a horizontal Showa pinball relic
facts: Specialises in smart ball, a flat ancestor of pachinko played for over 50 years
A 100-yen coin buys 25 balls; hitting numbered holes wins more
Counts as the only dedicated smart-ball hall in Osaka, perhaps in Japan
Adults only, as smart ball ranks legally alongside pachinko
w: Pachinko's flatter, slower grandparent, played on tilted tables where you flick balls into numbered holes, survives in one Osaka parlour, maybe the last in Japan. Drop 100 yen, get 25 balls, chase the clatter. Smoke-stained, fluorescent, pointless. They card you at the door, since the law counts it as gambling. Showa, on tilt.

n: Den Den Town and Ota Road
hook: Postwar electronics black market mutated into an otaku labyrinth
facts: Grew from a postwar black market in electronics parts, Osaka's answer to Akihabara
Pivoted in the 1990s from appliances to anime, manga, games and figures
A backstreet nicknamed Ota Road packs tiny specialist shops
Streets close yearly for the cosplay-heavy Nipponbashi Street Festa
w: Born as a postwar black market in radio valves and salvaged wiring, this grid sold appliances for decades, then in the 1990s the trade flipped to anime, manga and figures. A backstreet christened Ota Road crams the smallest, strangest shops. Once a year it shuts for a cosplay parade. Osaka's electric town, rewired for obsession.

hook: Japan's largest Koreatown, grown from a postwar black market
facts: Among the largest Koreatowns in Japan, centred on Miyuki-dori
Settled from the 1920s by Korean migrants, many from Jeju Island
A vast postwar black market here hardened into a permanent community
A Korea Town history museum opened nearby in 2023
w: From the 1920s, migrants from Jeju Island settled this corner of Ikuno; after the war a black market here set, like concrete, into Japan's biggest Korean quarter. Miyuki-dori still sells kimchi by the tub, chijimi off the griddle, hanbok in the window. A 2023 museum tells the harder history. Korea, three generations deep in Osaka.

hook: Osaka's oldest kissaten, three generations of home-roasted coffee and doughnuts
facts: Trading near Honmachi since 1921, reckoned the oldest kissaten in Osaka
Run by the same family across three generations
Known for home-roasted beans and homemade doughnuts
Offers original blends including a so-called Hyakunen, hundred-year, coffee
w: Reckoned the oldest kissaten in Osaka, pouring since 1921 and still in the founding family's hands three generations on. The draw stays simple: beans roasted in-house, doughnuts made on site, a house blend they cheerfully call hundred-year coffee. No latte art, no wifi sermons. A century of the same dark cup, in the canyons of Honmachi.
